title: Building in public with AI — what I learned running my own
dek: I didn't just write about AI this quarter — I ran it. A system built for my own work: 155 sessions, zero fabricated facts, verification built in from the start.

title: Aging happens fast now — before the systems are ready
dek: Bangladesh's over-60s nearly double by 2050 with no home-care model; the US market tops $250B. Elder care is the next market almost nobody's building yet.

title: The authenticity dividend - your last real moat
dek: In a feed drowning in AI sameness, the scarce thing is proof a person made it. Notes on trust — from Dhaka's markets to the backlash against AI slop.

title: Voice-first AI for the next billion
dek: The last billion online won't type — they'll talk. Voice skips the two barriers keeping AI from those who need it most: literacy and the keyboard.

title: The slow pandemic — antimicrobial resistance
dek: Antimicrobial resistance may kill 39 million by 2050 — three a minute, South Asia hardest hit. A behavior and communication problem, and so it's neglected.

title: AI can translate words, not localize culture
dek: AI dubbing cuts localization cost 70-90%; translation is nearly free. But translating words isn't localizing culture — and that gap is where judgment wins.

title: The pharmacy is the front door of care
dek: In Bangladesh care begins at the pharmacist; in the US ~50M live in a pharmacy desert. Same underused node — the pharmacy as health's real front door.

title: Commerce runs on chat, not storefronts
dek: Bangladesh runs one of the world's largest F-commerce ecosystems — 300,000+ sellers working from a chat thread. People buy from a person, not a store.

title: The dual-market operator's edge
dek: Most operators compete in one market. I read two — the US and Bangladesh — daily. Sixteen years taught me that bridge is a position no one else can occupy.

title: The nutrition transition: the environment is the disease
dek: Bangladesh now carries undernutrition and diabetes at once; the US drowns in the same processed calories. The food environment, not willpower, is the disease.

title: The model's blind spot is your opportunity
dek: AI models learn from a lopsided web. Every language and market it underrepresents is where models are weak — and where you can win. The gap is the opening.

title: Femtech: health's clearest open market
dek: Women's health is worth tens of billions, yet barely 4% of biopharma R&D addressed it. The under-served half of the population is health's clearest open market.

title: Create the category, don't compete in one
dek: Positioning wins a slot someone else defined. The stronger move: design the category itself — name the problem, set the terms, become the default answer.

title: The remittance-to-health pipeline
dek: Bangladesh took in a record $35.56B in remittances while ~70M face health hardship. Connect them: the country's biggest untapped health-financing rail.

title: The mosquito economy — dengue is a climate problem now
dek: Bangladesh logged 102,000+ dengue cases last year; 2026 is climbing. It's no longer only ours — local dengue is turning up in the US. Climate rewrote the map.

title: The call-center cliff and AI voice agents
dek: AI voice agents are coming for the call-center economy — Bangladesh built ~$850M and 85,000 jobs on it. Can we move people up the value chain fast enough?

title: Trust in the pill — the counterfeit-medicine problem
dek: One in ten medicines in poorer countries is substandard or fake — a trust collapse you engineer out of the supply. Rich-world shortages open the same door.

title: Measure the number you're avoiding
dek: Every team measures something. The winning ones measure the number they'd rather not look at — and decide what to count before they start, not after.

title: The quiet epidemic — tobacco and the new nicotine
dek: Tobacco kills ~158,000 a year in Bangladesh — one in five deaths; the US fight moved to youth vaping. Same economy, opposite framing — shaming never wins it.

title: Roads are a health crisis we refuse to own
dek: Bangladesh had its deadliest road year in 2025 — 9,000+ lives — while the US cut its toll. Same machine, opposite paths: one treats road death as designable.

title: When the buyer is an AI, not a person
dek: Google's "Buy for me" and ChatGPT Shopping mean the next shopper is an agent, not a person. Agentic commerce may hit $3-5T — few brands are built for it.

title: The cold chain is the product, not the pill
dek: US drug shortages hit 223 in early 2026, lasting five-plus years. For medicines, logistics is the health outcome — and whoever owns the last cold inch wins.

title: Pricing is positioning, not a spreadsheet
dek: Your price is the loudest thing your brand says. In cash markets where every taka is felt, pricing is your sharpest positioning decision — not a spreadsheet.

title: The $200M question — where to spend it on health
dek: The World Bank put $200M into Bangladesh's primary care. Given real money, where does a health dollar actually change an outcome? My answer isn't a shiny app.

title: Clean hands, still — the cheapest health tech we underuse
dek: Bangladesh hit 98% water access, yet arsenic poisons ~50M; the US is still pulling 9M lead pipes. Water and hygiene: the highest-return health tech, underused.

title: The lab is the wedge — diagnostics-led care
dek: In cash-pay markets the cheapest way into care isn't a doctor — it's a test. I built care that led with the lab, the model richer markets rediscover at home.

title: Air quality is a health emergency nobody owns
dek: Dhaka's air ranks among the world's worst — PM2.5 ~13x the WHO limit — and US wildfire smoke made the threat American. High-leverage, and no one owns it.

title: Financing the uninsured — the missing rail in health
dek: In Bangladesh, out-of-pocket costs are ~68.5% of health spending; ~70M face hardship. The fix isn't a clinic — it's a payment rail I built at national scale.

title: The first 1,000 days — the highest-ROI window in health
dek: Bangladesh cut maternal deaths 79%; the US is the only developed country where they rise. Conception to age two is where a health dollar does the most work.

title: Frugal innovation as a design philosophy
dek: The West calls it "frugal innovation." In Bangladesh we called it getting it done. The design principles of 2026 are what scarcity taught us years ago.

title: The last mile - health tech's untapped value
dek: Most health tech serves the first mile — the hospital. The untapped value is the last mile: the person far from care, reached where they are.

title: Clarity is an intervention, not polish
dek: We treat clarity as polish. It isn't. Confusing health guidance has a body count and a price tag — clarity is itself a health intervention.

title: The personal-brand playbook for the markets everyone ignores
dek: The most underrated growth asset in 2026 isn't a company brand — it's a person's. And the biggest opening to build one is where Western creators aren't looking.

title: The trust cold-start in two-sided health platforms
dek: Every platform faces the same first problem: nobody comes because nobody's there. In health it's worse — the currency isn't supply and demand, it's trust.

title: The workforce crisis and the community-health-worker answer
dek: America's health system is short of people, not technology. The answer is a model Bangladesh has run for decades — move tasks to those closest to the community.

title: The consent problem - health AI's real bottleneck
dek: Every debate about health AI fixates on accuracy. What actually stalls it is trust by design — consent, privacy, and provenance — the layer nobody demos.

title: Everyone says "AI-powered" - position on the job
dek: When every booth claims the same edge, the phrase means nothing. Positioning must move where copycats can't follow — the job the customer is hiring you to do.

title: Prevention-first — the design window that closes
dek: The US didn't choose sick-care — it backed into what the money rewarded. A market still building its system has an option the US lost: put prevention first.

title: How mental health became the premium category
dek: Investors once treated mental health as charity. In 2026 a mental-health app sold for $865M. The demand was always there — someone designed around the barrier.

title: After the scribe: where health AI goes next
dek: The AI scribe was the easy win. The real 2026 question is what AI does after the note — in the back office where the money and the misery live.

title: Stop throwing away what you publish
dek: The most wasteful thing in content isn't a bad idea — it's a good one you published once and dropped. Atomize a few durable assets instead of churning posts.

title: Telehealth's second act — care that comes to you
dek: Telehealth's first act was pandemic video for the waiting room. The second act is less a video call and more care delivered to your home, on your schedule.

title: Shame is a design flaw — not a personal failing
dek: Shame feels like clarity but works like a wall. In health comms, stigma is a barrier you design around — channel, framing, segmentation — not lecture away.

title: Provenance over volume - AI's next health win
dek: The AI health rush chases the wrong verb. Everyone races to generate more; the scarce thing was never volume — it's proving what's true and where it came from.

title: Retention is the real growth engine
dek: Acquisition costs rose ~60% in five years. Winners obsess over the leaky bucket — cash markets taught me retention, not reach, is the growth engine.

title: When patients pay cash, everything about the product changes
dek: In the US, health products are built for a payer. In most of the world the patient pays — and that fact is a design discipline the US is drifting toward.

title: A hotline on a bar of soap - distribution wins
dek: The best health channel I ever used wasn't an app or an ad — it was a bar of soap. Putting a clinic inside a product people owned took ~3,000 calls a day.

title: AI for the two-person Dhaka business
dek: Everyone tells the enterprise AI story. The one that matters: a two-person Dhaka shop running like a twenty-person one — an SME's first affordable team.

title: When nobody trusts anybody, trust is the growth strategy
dek: In low-trust categories, trust isn't a brand value — it's the growth engine. From moving 10 million onto digital payments where trust was the entire barrier.

title: The middle is disappearing: digital health's $4B quarter
dek: Digital health raised $4B last quarter and 12 companies took 59%. What that concentration and "AI as table stakes" mean for everyone who isn't a megadeal.

title: GLP-1s: the drug was never the hard part
dek: Only ~12% of Americans take a GLP-1, and most who start quit. Pharma solved the biology; behavior and adherence decide the outcome — the layer I've built on.

title: The agentic solo operator: a team of one
dek: Agentic AI removed the ceiling on what one operator can ship. Field notes from running a full growth engine solo — where judgment, not labor, is the limit.

title: The pendulum swings back to brand
dek: Performance marketers report diminishing returns, ad spend passed $1T, and AI floods every channel with sameness. The scarce asset: a brand people trust.

title: Where health AI actually lands by 2027 — a builder's bet
dek: Adoption is done. Three bets on where health AI actually lands by 2027 — people over models, judgment, and plumbing — each grounded in work I've shipped.

title: I rebuilt my website to be read by AI, not just Google
dek: 80% of searches now end without a click. Field notes on optimizing for answer engines — structured data, llms.txt, and becoming the source AI actually cites.

title: The healthtech leapfrog Bangladesh already made
dek: Bangladesh jumped from cash straight to mobile money. In health tech the same leapfrog is the opening — build access-first, not a copy of the US. I've run it.

title: Measles is back — a trust problem, not a supply problem
dek: The US passed 2,200 measles cases in 2026 — a trust problem, not supply. Why messengers beat facts, from a soap-packet hotline in Dhaka to a US campus campaign.

title: Bangla-first AI: the market the giants keep skipping
dek: Bengali is a top-seven world language and barely a fraction of the web. A builder's dual-market case for the opportunity the giants overlook.

title: Health communication that changes behavior
dek: Field notes on segmentation, friction, framing and measurement — the principles behind campaigns that move behavior, not just awareness.
